competitors.name: You
competitors.rules: Your real Robinhood account, rebased to the shared origin so it's comparable. Performance only — holdings and trades are deliberately not published.

competitors.name: congress_mirror
competitors.rules: Mirrors the most successful members of Congress: ranks filers by disclosed-trade excess return (kadoa STOCK Act mirror), then buys the names the top 10 disclosed purchasing within 120 days, weighted by how many bought each. Traded on the disclosure date — weeks after their actual fill.

competitors.name: S&P 500
competitors.rules: Buys SPY with the whole book on the first forward session and holds — the market baseline.

trade_log.reason: buy & hold: all-in on day one

competitors.name: momentum_breakout
competitors.rules: Buy a 20-day-high breakout confirmed by above-average volume; exit when price closes below its 20-day SMA. Few big winners, many small losers.

trade_log.reason: fill to cap: breakout > 20d high on volume
fill to cap: breakout > 20d high on volume

competitors.name: blended_momo_rsi
competitors.rules: Momentum breakout, but skip entries already overbought (RSI ≥ 70); exit on trend break or RSI > 75. Avoids chasing extended moves.

trade_log.reason: fill to cap: breakout, RSI 64 not overbought
fill to cap: breakout, RSI 66 not overbought

competitors.name: mean_reversion
competitors.rules: Buy when RSI(14) < 30 (oversold); sell when RSI(14) > 70 (overbought). Trades less, wins more often, smaller edge.

competitors.name: llm_voters
competitors.rules: Allocates across the swarm's top vote-getters (weight ∝ vote share, capped at 60%); rebalanced each tick.

competitors.name: deep_research_analyst
competitors.rules: Holds the analyst's target weights; rebalanced each tick from a fresh research report (web + Robinhood data).

competitors.name: mirofish_real
competitors.rules: Real-MiroFish: persona agents with memory interact over rounds, each ranking its best ideas; the panel's rank-weighted consensus (top 5 names, weight ∝ Borda points) rebalanced each tick.

analyst.framework: Claude for Financial Services — equity-research methodology (/screen, /sector, /comps, /catalysts, /thesis). Data: Robinhood fundamentals + web_search (no paid-vendor MCP connectors).
analyst.sizing: 0.14 top weight — anchor the book in regime-favored aero/defense industrials making fresh 52-wk highs; rotate the freed laggard capital into the same cyclical-value sleeve.

regime.label: Hawkish hold + AI memory super-cycle
regime.note: 06-17 FOMC held at 3.50-3.75% under new chair Warsh but the dot plot flipped to a HIKE bias (median end-2026 3.8% vs 3.4% prior, the cut erased; 9 of 18 see a hike; traders eye October) on Iran-war inflation. That favors energy/industrials/materials/financials over long-duration growth. Overlaid on a memory super-cycle: Micron's blowout print is the winner; the same memory-cost squeeze is the cost-side villain forcing hardware OEMs (AAPL -6%) to raise prices.

analyst.thesis: What changed since 6/24: the value/quality rotation I started is now working AND the one growth name I held into its catalyst paid off — so I lean into the winners and cut the dead weight. The macro is unambiguously hawkish (Fed dot plot now prices at least one hike, Iran-war inflation), which rewards cyclical value with pricing power: industrials, financials, materials. I concentrate the book there where regime tailwind, momentum, and relative strength all agree: GE + CAT (industrials at fresh 52-wk highs; CAT's Power & Energy turbines lever the data-center power theme) and BAC + JPM (cheap/quality banks at 52-wk highs; a higher-for-longer path supports NIM). I keep a trimmed MU core: its Q3 was a genuine blowout (rev +279%, EPS +998%, HBM sold out, HBM4 ramping for NVIDIA) and at 23.7x P/E it isn't optically expensive, but it's a cyclical memory name at a $1.37T cap up ~700% in a year that swung $1,136-$1,255 intraday on the print — so I lock part of the +15.7% pop and ride a smaller position rather than chase. I deliberately SKIP energy despite the regime narrative: XOM and COP are both well off their March highs with no momentum, so buying them would fight the tape. ~42% cash held against the October-hike risk.

evidence.point: FOMC voted unanimously to hold the funds rate at 3.50-3.75%; the median end-2026 dot rose to 3.8% from 3.4%, erasing the prior cut, with nine of eighteen participants expecting at least one hike and traders pricing a possible October hike.
In 2026 investors favored sectors with stronger pricing power, steadier demand, or direct energy exposure: energy, industrials, materials, and utilities.

Micron Q3 FY26 (reported 6/24): revenue more than quadrupled (analysts modeled ~$35B, +279%) with EPS growth forecast near +998%; its entire 2026 HBM output is sold out and contracted, HBM4 for NVIDIA's Vera Rubin began shipping in March and is ramping ~2x the HBM3E pace; stock +15% after hours, market cap past $1T.

MU trades at a 23.7x P/E and $1.37T market cap on 6/25 despite a ~700% one-year run — the multiple is modest only because earnings exploded; it opened $1,234, hit $1,255 (fresh 52-wk high) and dropped to $1,136 intraday, closing $1,213.52 (+15.7% on the day).

Apple fell ~6% to ~$275 on 6/25 after a rare mid-cycle hardware price hike (MacBook Air $1,099->$1,299, 14" MacBook Pro $1,699->$1,999); CEO Cook called the memory-supply squeeze 'unavoidable' and a 'hundred-year flood' — the cost side of the same shortage lifting Micron.
GE Aerospace: P/E 45.1, made a fresh 52-wk high of $379.67 on 6/25 (52-wk low $243.34), +17.0% over the last 20 sessions vs SPY -2.3%.

Caterpillar: P/E 49.5, fresh 52-wk high $1,057 on 6/25, +16.1% over 20 sessions and +10.6% over the last 5 (accelerating); Power & Energy segment supplies gas turbines/reciprocating engines into power generation.
BAC: P/E 14.3, P/B 1.49, dividend 1.9%, fresh 52-wk high $59.19 on 6/25, +13.9% over 20 sessions.
JPM: P/E 16.0, dividend 1.77%, fresh 52-wk high $343.45 on 6/25, +12.0% over 20 sessions.

Energy is regime-favored but momentum disagrees: XOM at ~$136 sits well below its 52-wk high of $176.41 (set in March), COP at ~$106 below its $135.87 high; neither appears in the universe's top-15 by 20-day momentum.

risks: MU is a cyclical memory name at peak earnings, $1.37T cap, up ~700%/yr; a sell-the-news fade (it already round-tripped $1,255->$1,136 intraday on the print) or a memory-cycle rollover could erase the gain — the trim mitigates but does not remove this.
GE (45x) and CAT (49x) are priced for perfection; an October hike that backs up the long end would compress high-P/E industrial multiples even with intact fundamentals.
Financial concentration: BAC + JPM = 22% of equity; the higher-for-longer thesis helps NIM, but a credit event or a growth scare hits banks together.
Regime risk: if the Fed actually hikes and the curve re-prices, even cyclical value can derate broadly — the ~42% cash buffer is the hedge.
Skipping energy leaves the book under-hedged to an Iran-war oil spike / inflation surprise, the exact tail the regime warns about.

data_examined.label: June 2026 FOMC decision and Summary of Economic Projections (rate hold, hike-biased dots)
Micron Q3 FY26 earnings (rev +279%, EPS +998%, HBM sold out, HBM4 ramp)
Apple 6/25 price-hike selloff and the 'hundred-year flood' memory squeeze
Robinhood fundamentals (P/E, P/B, 52-wk range, market cap, dividend) for GE/CAT/DE/BAC/JPM/MU/XOM/COP/TGT/MRNA on 2026-06-25
20-day / 5-day momentum and relative strength vs SPY across the full universe

rationale.GE: Anchor (0.14). [DATA] Fresh 52-wk high $379.67 on 6/25, +17% over 20 sessions vs SPY -2.3%, aero/defense squarely in the regime's favored industrials sleeve. [DATA] P/E is 45x, so this is a quality-momentum hold, not a value buy. [ASSUMED] Commercial-aero aftermarket + defense backlog is the steadiest large-cap industrial demand stream into a hawkish, inflationary tape, which is why it's the anchor rather than a cyclical with order-book risk.
rationale.CAT: New initiation (0.12). [DATA] P/E 49.5, fresh 52-wk high $1,057 on 6/25, +16% 20d and +10.6% over the last 5 sessions — the strongest accelerating momentum among the industrial finalists. [DATA] Its Power & Energy segment makes gas turbines and reciprocating engines for power generation. [ASSUMED] That ties CAT to the data-center power build-out, giving it a secular leg on top of the cyclical/infrastructure demand the regime rewards; I size it below GE because 49x leaves no room for a momentum stumble.
rationale.BAC: Add (0.10 -> 0.12). [DATA] P/E 14.3, P/B 1.49, 1.9% yield, fresh 52-wk high $59.19 on 6/25, +13.9% 20d, +0.8% on the day. [ASSUMED] The cleanest cheap-value expression of a higher-for-longer rate path: a hike-biased Fed widens net interest margin, and at 1.5x book the downside is far better protected than the 45-49x industrials. Existing position, working — lean in.
rationale.MU: Trim and hold (0.13 -> 0.10). [DATA] Q3 was a genuine blowout: rev +279%, EPS +998%, HBM sold out and contracted, HBM4 ramping ~2x HBM3E for NVIDIA; stock +15.7% on 6/25 to a $1.37T cap, and at 23.7x P/E it isn't optically rich. [DATA] But it swung $1,255->$1,136 intraday on the print. [ASSUMED] The thesis (HBM scarcity) has now largely played out and is priced; this is a cyclical semi near peak earnings up ~700% in a year, so discipline says lock part of the pop and ride a smaller core rather than chase — exactly the 'let winners run but bank the spike' lesson I missed earlier.
rationale.JPM: New initiation (0.10). [DATA] P/E 16.0, 1.77% yield, fresh 52-wk high $343.45 on 6/25, +12% 20d. [ASSUMED] The highest-quality U.S. bank and the 'real ballast' my last reflection said the book lacked: it gives a second rate-tailwind leg with best-in-class capital/credit, diversifying the financial sleeve beyond BAC without reaching for beta. Sized at 0.10 so the two banks together stay a controlled 22%.

reflection.looking_back: Going into 6/24 I rotated toward value/quality — GE 0.15, BAC 0.10, TGT 0.12 — while holding MU 0.13 through its after-hours print and keeping MRNA 0.08, ~42% cash. The decisive call was holding MU into earnings: it reported a blowout (rev +279%, EPS +998%, HBM sold out) and gapped +15.7% on 6/25, by far the biggest contributor (~+2% to the book) and the first positive-alpha day in a while against a flat SPY. The anchors confirmed the rotation — GE +1.5% and BAC +0.8% both made fresh 52-wk highs.
worked: Holding MU through the print instead of de-risking it: +15.7% on 6/25 (~+2% book contribution) — the single best decision of the tick.
The industrials/financials rotation: GE +1.5% and BAC +0.8%, each at a fresh 52-wk high on 6/25, vindicating the move out of high-multiple growth into regime-aligned cyclical value.
missed: TGT was dead weight: -1.2% on 6/25, no momentum (not in the universe top-15 by 20d), and discretionary retail isn't a sleeve a hawkish/inflation regime rewards — it diluted the MU win.
MRNA was the same mistake in biotech form: -1.2% on 6/25, fading off its 6/18 high, P/E -7.4 (unprofitable), no near-term catalyst and no regime fit — an idiosyncratic name I kept too long.
reflection.adjustment: Bank the MU spike by trimming it (0.13->0.10), cut both laggards (TGT and MRNA to zero), and concentrate the freed capital into the cyclical-value sleeve that's actually working — initiate CAT (industrials + data-center power) and JPM (quality bank) alongside the GE/BAC anchors.

swarm.note: Live swarm — 150 fish, each a unique trader profile shown its own random ~20-name slice of the watchlist (with recent headlines), voted via OpenRouter. Independent-voter election; dissenters (ringed) chose CASH.

live.note: Connected to the Agentic cash account •••• — currently unfunded ($0). Deposit into this specific account to trade the real $100. No agent orders placed yet.

rules.momentum_breakout: Buy a 20-day-high breakout confirmed by above-average volume; exit when price closes below its 20-day SMA. Few big winners, many small losers.
rules.mean_reversion: Buy when RSI(14) < 30 (oversold); sell when RSI(14) > 70 (overbought). Trades less, wins more often, smaller edge.
rules.blended_momo_rsi: Momentum breakout, but skip entries already overbought (RSI ≥ 70); exit on trend break or RSI > 75. Avoids chasing extended moves.
rules.deep_research_analyst: Holds the analyst's target weights; rebalanced each tick from a fresh research report (web + Robinhood data).
rules.llm_voters: Allocates across the swarm's top vote-getters (weight ∝ vote share, capped at 60%); rebalanced each tick.
rules.mirofish_real: Real-MiroFish: persona agents with memory interact over rounds, each ranking its best ideas; the panel's rank-weighted consensus (top 5 names, weight ∝ Borda points) rebalanced each tick.
rules.congress_mirror: Mirrors the most successful members of Congress: ranks filers by disclosed-trade excess return (kadoa STOCK Act mirror), then buys the names the top 10 disclosed purchasing within 120 days, weighted by how many bought each. Traded on the disclosure date — weeks after their actual fill.

sources.name: Standings, curves, decision trail (LIVE)
sources.detail: The forward paper test — every competitor trades a $100 book the same way, advanced one trading day per tick (rule strategies via tick.py / bot/engine.py; agents via run_agents.py / bot/paper.py), 5 bps slippage, −15% stop. Young — it grows one session per tick.

sources.name: Backtest reference
sources.detail: A separate Dec–Jun walk-forward backtest (2026-01-02–2026-06-25, in-sample, mostly-bull) is shown inside each rule strategy's detail for context only — it is NOT the live board.

sources.name: Research analyst
sources.detail: Agent-driven on the Claude Code plan: web_search + Robinhood data → one report (state/analyst.json) with target weights per tick. Sources link from each evidence row.

sources.name: Swarm vote
sources.detail: Independent-voter election: 150 unique-profile cheap models (DeepSeek/Gemini/Llama/Haiku via OpenRouter) each read one shared briefing and return a single ballot; tallied in bot/swarm.py. No interaction between fish.

sources.name: Real-MiroFish swarm
sources.detail: Social-simulation swarm (bot/mirofish.py): persona agents with memory interact over multiple rounds — each re-ranks its best ideas seeing its own prior view and its neighbours' latest — so a consensus forms via peer influence (the opposite of the independent swarm). The panel's rank-weighted (Borda) consensus becomes a multi-name book (top 5 names, weight ∝ points). Tiered cost (cheap/default/qwen); seeded from the briefing + optional world-events news.

sources.name: Live account
sources.detail: Robinhood MCP, Agentic cash account ••••, via get_portfolio / get_equity_positions / get_equity_orders.

sources.name: Display scale
sources.detail: The real paper books are $100 each; every dollar and share figure on this site is shown ×100 (a $10,000 book) so holdings read in whole shares and dollars. Per-share prices and percentages are unscaled.
